**Beijing Chengfeng Football Club** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 北京橙丰; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Běijīng Chéngfēng*) was a professional Chinese [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club that last participated in the [Chinese League One](/wiki/Chinese_League_One "Chinese League One") under licence from the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") (CFA). The team was based in [Fengtai District](/wiki/Fengtai_District "Fengtai District"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") and their home stadium was the [Beijing Fengtai Stadium](/wiki/Beijing_Fengtai_Stadium "Beijing Fengtai Stadium") that has a seating capacity of 31,043. Their last majority shareholder was Chinese property developers of shopping centers Renhe Commercial Holdings Company Limited.

The club was founded in [Pudong District](/wiki/Pudong_District "Pudong District"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") on 3 February 1995 and were originally known as Shanghai Pudong before they made their debut in the third tier of China's football league pyramid in the 1995 season. They would work their way up to the top tier while changing name to accommodate their sponsors. In the 2006 season the club would relocate the team to [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi") and rename themselves Xi'an Chanba International, however by the 2012 season, the club relocated this time to [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), and changed their name to Guizhou Renhe.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the 2016 season the club relocated the team to [Fengtai District](/wiki/Fengtai_District "Fengtai District"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), and changed their name to Beijing Renhe. Throughout the club's history their greatest achievement has been winning the [2013 Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/2013_Chinese_FA_Cup "2013 Chinese FA Cup") while the highest position they have ever finished was second within the 2003 season.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beijing_Chengfeng_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/85/Shanghai_Pudong_Football_Club.svg/250px-Shanghai_Pudong_Football_Club.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Pudong_Football_Club.svg)

logo of Shanghai Pudong Football Club in 1995

The club was founded on 3 February 1995 in [Pudong District](/wiki/Pudong_District "Pudong District"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") to take part in the recently formed fully professional football league system and they started at the bottom of the football pyramid in the third division, where they named themselves Shanghai Pudong. Playing in all blue in their debut season, they would immediately taste success when they won the division title and promotion to the second tier.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The following seasons, however, saw the team languish within the division until they brought in [Xu Genbao](/wiki/Xu_Genbao "Xu Genbao") to manage the side at the beginning of the 2000 season and would make the club promotion contenders. Under Xu Genbao's leadership, they didn't have to wait long to win promotion when they would go on to win the division title at the end of the season and a chance to play in the top tier.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Under the ownership of Shanghai Yungtay Engineering and COSCO Real Estate, the club rebranded themselves with a new blue and white striped football kit. They were big spenders who wanted to achieve immediate success by bringing in established Chinese internationals such as [Cheng Yaodong](/wiki/Cheng_Yaodong "Cheng Yaodong"), [Jiang Jin](/wiki/Jiang_Jin "Jiang Jin") and particularly [Wu Chengying](/wiki/Wu_Chengying "Wu Chengying") who set a Chinese transfer fee record of 13,000,000 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi"). This saw them become genuine title contenders and under their new manager Cheng Yaodong, they would fight for the league title with [Shanghai Greenland Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Greenland_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Greenland Shenhua F.C.") and only come second by a single point at the end of the [2003 season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_2003 "Chinese Jia-A League 2003").[[4]](#cite_note-4) On 13 June 2012, [it was discovered by the police the real reason](/wiki/2003%E2%80%932009_Chinese_football_match-fixing_scandals#Shanghai_International_vs_Tianjin_TEDA_in_2003 "2003–2009 Chinese football match-fixing scandals") the team lost the 2003 title was because the club's players [Shen Si](/wiki/Shen_Si "Shen Si"), [Qi Hong](/wiki/Qi_Hong "Qi Hong"), [Jiang Jin](/wiki/Jiang_Jin "Jiang Jin") and [Li Ming](/wiki/Li_Ming_(footballer,_born_1975) "Li Ming (footballer, born 1975)") took a bribe from former [Tianjin TEDA](/wiki/Tianjin_TEDA_F.C. "Tianjin TEDA F.C.") general manager Yang Yifeng to lose their 30 November 2003 game, which saw all offending participants fined and jailed for their crimes.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/94/Shanghai_Yungtay_Football_Club.svg/250px-Shanghai_Yungtay_Football_Club.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Yungtay_Football_Club.svg)

logo of Shanghai Yungtay Football Clubin 2005

The owners could not maintain the level of spending that they had done and the team's results would start to slip. Finding that they could not compete with Shanghai Shenhua and in the 2005 season, they had to face additional competition in [Shanghai Zobon](/wiki/Shanghai_Zobon_F.C. "Shanghai Zobon F.C."), the team decided to move to Xi'an after months of speculation. With the newly branded team known as Shanghai International, they would start to move away from the previous [Yuanshen Stadium](/wiki/Yuanshen_Stadium "Yuanshen Stadium") in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") to the [Shaanxi Province Stadium](/wiki/Shaanxi_Province_Stadium "Shaanxi Province Stadium") in [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), [Shaanxi Province](/wiki/Shaanxi_Province "Shaanxi Province") and rename themselves Xi'an Chanba International by 2006. In 2007, their ownership was transferred to Baorong Investment and it was during this period that the club would start to experiment with a new yellow football kit. This would surprisingly seem to work when the club looked as if they were title contenders once more during the 2008 season, however their title hopes quickly faded and the team eventually finished fifth. The following season, however, would see the team languish near the bottom of the table and Cheng Yaodong decided to resign, which would see former Chinese national football coach [Zhu Guanghu](/wiki/Zhu_Guanghu "Zhu Guanghu") come in and guide the team away from the relegation zone.

At the beginning of the [2010 season](/wiki/2010_Chinese_Super_League "2010 Chinese Super League"), [Dai Yongge](/wiki/Dai_Yongge "Dai Yongge") and the Renhe Commercial Holdings Company started to invest heavily within the club. This would see the club bring in Chinese internationals [Sun Jihai](/wiki/Sun_Jihai "Sun Jihai"), [Zhao Xuri](/wiki/Zhao_Xuri "Zhao Xuri"), [Qu Bo](/wiki/Qu_Bo_(footballer) "Qu Bo (footballer)") and [Mao Jianqing](/wiki/Mao_Jianqing "Mao Jianqing") into the team. However, despite the signings, the club struggled within the league and Zhu Guanghu left the club while three time Chinese league winner [Milorad Kosanović](/wiki/Milorad_Kosanovi%C4%87 "Milorad Kosanović") replaced him.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Milorad Kosanović's reign at the club was unsuccessful and he was soon replaced by [Slobodan Santrač](/wiki/Slobodan_Santra%C4%8D "Slobodan Santrač"). After a poor string of results, Slobodan Santrač was fired and former Chinese international manager [Gao Hongbo](/wiki/Gao_Hongbo "Gao Hongbo") came into the club while it languished in mid-table throughout much of the [2011 season](/wiki/2011_Chinese_Super_League "2011 Chinese Super League").[[7]](#cite_note-7) After another disappointing season, Dai Yongge would start to get frustrated at the team's lack of success and decided to take advantage of [Guiyang](/wiki/Guiyang "Guiyang")'s government promise of the recently developed [Guiyang Olympic Centre](/wiki/Guiyang_Olympic_Centre "Guiyang Olympic Centre") for the club, and with Renhe Commercial Holdings Company having better business connections within Guiyang, the club decided that it would move the team, which has recently made them one of the best supported teams in China.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The [2012 season](/wiki/2012_Chinese_Super_League "2012 Chinese Super League") saw Guizhou have a successful year, with the club achieving fourth place and gaining entry into its first [AFC Champions League](/wiki/2013_AFC_Champions_League "2013 AFC Champions League").

The team's success continued as they qualified for the [2014 AFC Champions League](/wiki/2014_AFC_Champions_League "2014 AFC Champions League") as well, but got knocked out in the group stage both times they qualified. Their top achievements in this period included winning the [2014 Chinese FA Super Cup](/wiki/2014_Chinese_FA_Super_Cup "2014 Chinese FA Super Cup") and the [2013 Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/2013_Chinese_FA_Cup "2013 Chinese FA Cup"). In the [2015 season](/wiki/2015_Chinese_Super_League "2015 Chinese Super League") they were relegated to the [League One](/wiki/2016_China_League_One "2016 China League One"), but the team managed to advance back to the [Super League](/wiki/2018_Chinese_Super_League "2018 Chinese Super League") in 2018. In 2016 they moved from Guizhou to Beijing, becoming Beijing Renhe.[[9]](#cite_note-9) After one season where they placed eighth, in 2019 the club struggled to win games and found themselves in last place with a few rounds to go.

Having lost their [2020 China League One relegation play-off matches](/wiki/2020_China_League_One#Relegation_play-offs "2020 China League One") 2–3 on aggregate to [Jiangxi Liansheng](/wiki/Jiangxi_Liansheng_F.C. "Jiangxi Liansheng F.C."), Beijing Renhe were relegated to the [League Two](/wiki/2021_China_League_Two "2021 China League Two"). They changed their name to Beijing Chengfeng to meet [Chinese Football Association's "neutral name" requirement](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association#Club_name_neutralization "Chinese Football Association"), before the dissolution of the club on 29 March 2021.

## Crest and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beijing_Chengfeng_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Crest and colours")]

When the club originated their home colours would predominantly be blue until the club won promotion to the top tier and decided that they needed to differentiate themselves from their local rivals [Shanghai Greenland Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Greenland_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Greenland Shenhua F.C."), who also play in blue.[[12]](#cite_note-12) This saw them employ a blue and white stripe top at the beginning of the [2003 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_2003 "Chinese Jia-A League 2003") and a new crest design of a horse in front of a striped background which was directly inspired by [Juventus FC](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") own logo.[[13]](#cite_note-13) When the club was bought out by Baorong Investments who moved the club to [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an") they decided that the club should use a new yellow top by the beginning of the [2008 league season](/wiki/2008_Chinese_Super_League "2008 Chinese Super League") and a new crest of a wolf was employed.[[14]](#cite_note-14) When the Renhe Commercial Holdings Company bought a majority within the club they wanted to try out a new all black kit during the [2011 league season](/wiki/2011_Chinese_Super_League "2011 Chinese Super League"), however this colour did not last very long and when the company decided to move the club to [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou") the club decided they needed a new kit to signify this move and launched an all orange kit at the beginning of the [2012 league season](/wiki/2012_Chinese_Super_League "2012 Chinese Super League").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beijing_Chengfeng_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

When the club was founded in Shanghai they decided to take advantage of the [1994](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1994 "Chinese Jia-A League 1994") Chinese football league professionalism reforms that allowed more than one club in each city. With Shanghai Shenhua already established within the city the potential for China's first top-flight city derby emerged. On 9 March 2002 the first top-flight city derby became a reality when they met in a league game, which saw the club win 2–0 away to Shenhua in front of a sold out [Hongkou Football Stadium](/wiki/Hongkou_Football_Stadium "Hongkou Football Stadium"). Known as the Shanghai derby it would be the start of an intense but short rivalry between the two clubs, which reached its peak on the final day of the 2003 league season with both teams able to win the league title.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Shenhua won their game while the club surprisingly lost theirs to relegation fighting club [Tianjin TEDA](/wiki/Tianjin_TEDA_F.C. "Tianjin TEDA F.C."). This saw critics dispute the title win and it was eventually discovered that both teams had players and officials [match-fix games throughout the campaign](/wiki/2003%E2%80%932009_Chinese_football_match-fixing_scandals "2003–2009 Chinese football match-fixing scandals").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Shenhua would retrospectively lose their title while the club owners decided it was financially unviable to remain in Shanghai and relocated their team to [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), which effectively ended the rivalry.
